point_001: The model is evaluated on only one existing benchmark (HumanEval, with just 164 problems) and not on other publicly available coding datasets such as APPS or MBPP, limiting the breadth of evaluation.
point_002: Potential training data contamination is not investigated: since HumanEval problems may have been posted to GitHub, the authors should check whether the training set contains near-duplicate solutions.
point_003: The deduplication methodology is too weak—only exact duplicate files are removed, leaving many near-duplicate files that differ only in minor ways (e.g., header comments), which could inflate performance.
point_004: The BigPython dataset, which appears responsible for much of the performance gains, is described only minimally; more detail on its construction, composition, and statistics is needed.
point_005: There is insufficient ablation or analysis of hyperparameter choices and training data ordering (i.e., the sequential fine-tuning curriculum), with little justification provided for the values used.
point_006: The training datasets (BigPython in particular) are not released, limiting reproducibility and community use.
point_007: The practical utility of the Multi-Turn Programming Benchmark (MTPB) is questionable: prompts are often longer than the code they describe, the benchmark does not support back-and-forth correction, and it is unclear whether real users can provide such fine-grained, structured guidance without being able to write the code themselves.
point_008: MTPB lacks coverage of complex control-flow structures (e.g., loops, branches, recursion), and it is unclear how sub-tasks within such structures would be specified or evaluated.
point_009: The MTPB evaluation methodology is insufficiently explained: how test-case inputs are embedded in prompts, how multiple test cases are handled, and how inference and scoring work are not clearly described.
point_010: The claim that MTPB performance improves as a function of 'data size' is confounded because additional data is also more domain-specific (Python-focused), making it impossible to attribute gains solely to data quantity.
point_011: The relationship between MTPB and prior related work (e.g., SPoC by Kulal et al., 2019; Django dataset by Oda et al.) is not adequately discussed, making it hard to assess the novelty of the multi-turn benchmark.
point_012: Table 2 reports perplexity results only for CodeGen-Mono, omitting the other model variants; similarly, Table 3 compares only against GPT-Neo and GPT-J without including Codex, weakening the comparative analysis.
point_013: The description of which datasets each model variant (CodeGen-Multi vs. CodeGen-Mono) was trained on appears only late in the paper (Section 4.3 and appendix) rather than in the model description section, reducing clarity.
point_014: The multi-turn evaluation does not isolate the effect of correct intermediate code: results would be more informative if evaluated under the condition that all previous turns produced correct outputs.
